author: @eagleone5456
text: Depression is such an incredibly difficult war to wage. Who could have ever guessed? God Bless you Robert.

author: @sarune2

author: @seejayj
text: Sad you didn't keep learning with us.

author: @magrew66
text: When i was 8 my uncle Robbie asked me if i could do something in his garage, I said I couldn't, he said how do you know, have you tried? I said no. He said you can do nearly anything if you try. The most inspirational statement of my life. I am now a Fellow of engineering purely by that short conversation.

replies.author: @harleyAtdk
replies.text: Great story. It's always intrigued me how such short moments, that you mention, may cause ripple effects. When about 7 or 8 my mother bought me a computer-magazine, I probably expressed interest because of colorful front-page. That spiked an interest in all things computers, and from that came a career that's lasted several decades, from which came a salary that allowed me to solely finance a family of 4. All that from 'Mum, can I...?'. Thanks for recalling your anecdote.

replies.author: @townbell2248
replies.text: Thank you for sharing that❤ He was a beautiful soul!  If you ever want to share more stories about him, please do!

replies.author: @townbell2248
replies.text:
@magrew66 I emailed him about my ideas to create something. I dont know much about anything really but he took the time and sent me old patents that were similar to my idea. 

He didn’t know me and it meant so much that he took the time to respond to me ❤ he is very much missed.

author: @LeftBoot
text: Such a shame that Roberts main project wasn't his own mental health. 😔 🧠 🍄 RIP x

replies.author: @Ninsidhe
replies.text: He did- he made the best choice for _himself_ . Nobody is obligated to continue living if it has genuinely become a burden.

replies.author: @FilthyApe
replies.text: @NinsidheDefinitely shouldn’t glorify suicide

replies.author: @LeftBoot
replies.text: @Ninsidhe and what about the friends and family around him who loved him? How does it affect the rest of their lives?

replies.author: @timmyssweet
replies.text: @NinsidheI respect his decision, but I am concerned that stress, from all he was dealing with, overwhelmed him.  Never underestimate what stress can do to a person.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: HA I took that (30 to 50 pound roll of filament) as the weight for a second cause I guess I am tired, (and yes I am still catching up on those videos I have not watched, so I am just now getting around to this older video even though you have already left us @ThingingandTinkering ) :(

author: @Allwestconstruction
text:
Hiya mate, as you may remember I work on battery cells, I used to work on crystal cells, started back a little, you can look back and I charged some of these cells with a candle, also when i short these cells it can start out at 2MA and then go all the way up to 60MA after 10 min and then stay for a long time before going down , what i think is going on with these is some type of thermo electric cells, the heat from shorting it out is also charging it,
 I think it is also a galvanic cell but something else,  that it can create its own rise in amps 
CAN I get your take on it?

THANKS

replies.author: @Allwestconstruction
replies.text: The cool part is that you do not need any outside source of heat, the cell itself provides the heat, kind of like a self generating cell power

author: @manwithnoname3454
text: Love your channel! What would happen if you put two stirling engines one on each side of a tube furnace? These stirling engines could maybe run a 500 watt generator. If you can get the tube furnace to run both on 500 watts of power?

replies.author: @manwithnoname3454
replies.text: That's two 500 watt stirling engine generators, one 500 watt tube furnace.

author: @theodorekorehonen
text: I myself really enjoy the process of making things, sometimes even more than having the finished product. But as you said, everyone has different needs and goals. It also probably explains why I've got so many unfinished projects hahaha

author: @mulgerbill
text:
Thank you for introducing me to Tinkercad Robert, learning to use it has allowed me to overcome many of my own limitations and create actual designs for large projects rather than a handful of roughly sketched ideas to guide the muck about in the shed and hope the idea works once converted into a physical object. Only one of half a dozen or so so far has been something printable, a modification to a tool part that I always thought could be better and stronger. I got it printed by a mate for the cost of a bottle of something nice and it's working very well so far. 
But it could be better... It's time to look towards a printer of my own. Thanks again for the inspiration and guidance.

author: @BigelowInventions
text: I've been making my 3D models in Blender for the last 2 years and I now know that was a horrible mistake. I can't wait to learn these actual CAD programs. Thanks for the suggestions!

author: @richardglickman7203
text:
When I asked my question I had no idea how I would answer it, let alone you. I also didn’t expect such a quick response. 
I am definitely impressed with your response. Fast, complete, coherent, and enthusiastically encouraging.
Well done and thank you!

author: @Forensic1Man
text:
Hi Rob,
It’s hard to believe it’s been 18 months already! Have you crossed paths with anyone who shares your passion for teaching and diving into fascinating projects?
Now that you’ve become something of a celebrity, has anyone sparked your curiosity enough to collaborate—maybe even appear in one of your videos?
Just wondering what kind of creative energy you’ve been encountering lately.
Cheers, Brian

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I run into folks all the time who do that mate - and i have done collabs

author: @williamwells3026
text: You learn more in one failure than 10 successes.

replies.author: @theodorekorehonen
replies.text: I often start things knowing I don't have the skillset to succeed but by stumbling my way circuitously through it, I wind up learning quite a bit!

author: @some-other-guy
text: What i still don't understand, is why so many people just don't seem to have or even start to develop that philosophy. After doing all kinds of stuff (no 3D printing though) together with others, i mostly prefer to do that alone by myself now, because it feels so much more challenging to either find somebody who uses the same approach or to deal with the stress of someone who doesn't. I'm gonna have to reference this video next time i need a project partner.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I know - it seems like common sense and in a way it is - but not it is also not that common!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @grendel1960a
text: I can remember the man that wasnt even sure about CAD, who then went on to teach himself and made a darn good job of it too, as a more experienced CAD user, I still tend to work in fusion, it does what I want. the other thing i use is lightburn, as I do a lot on my laser cutter nowadays, recently I was asked if i could make a specific spacer for a water pump (component no longer manufactured) I had several goes trying to get it absolutely right on the 3d printer, wasnt happy with the result and went old school, turned some nylon on the lathe then milled out the necessary parts, i used the lasercutter to create a workholder to hold these thin nylon discs in the milling machine to cut the slots. whatever gets the job done is my motto. yes i will try the quick easy way first, if that doesnt work, i can happily do it another way.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think that is the best way to work mate - and yes I remember that dude too lol
